title: Living in Greenwich: Council Tax, Schools, Transport and Local Services Guide
excerpt: A practical guide to daily life in the Royal Borough of Greenwich, covering council tax, school options, transport networks, healthcare, and local amenities.
metaDescription: Complete guide to living in Greenwich. Council tax rates, best schools, transport links, NHS services, and amenities across the Royal Borough of Greenwich.
content:
A Practical Guide to Life in the Royal Borough of Greenwich

Whether you're considering moving to the Royal Borough of Greenwich or you've lived here for years, understanding the practical aspects of daily life — from council tax and schools to transport and healthcare — helps you make informed decisions about where to live and what to expect. This comprehensive guide covers everything you need to know about the essentials of Greenwich living.

Council Tax

How It Works

Council tax in Greenwich is set by the Royal Borough of Greenwich in conjunction with the Greater London Authority. The amount you pay depends on your property's council tax band, determined by its value in April 1991. Greenwich has properties across all bands, with the majority falling in bands C to E.

Current Rates

A Band D property in the Royal Borough of Greenwich currently pays approximately £1,700-£1,800 in total council tax (including the GLA precept). Greenwich's rates are broadly competitive with other South East London boroughs. Band A properties pay approximately two-thirds of the Band D rate, while Band H properties pay roughly double.

Variation Across the Borough

Council tax bands broadly reflect 1991 property values, so higher-value areas have higher average bands. Many properties in Blackheath and Greenwich Town fall within bands E to H, while Eltham properties are typically bands C to E, and Thamesmead and Plumstead properties often fall within bands A to D. This creates significant variation in annual council tax bills between different parts of the borough.

Discounts and Exemptions

Single-person households receive a 25% discount. Full-time students are exempt. Properties empty due to the owner's death receive a temporary exemption. The council offers a council tax reduction scheme for low-income residents. Disabled residents may qualify for a band reduction. Empty property premiums apply to long-term vacant homes — relevant for executors managing inherited properties.

Schools

Primary Schools

The Royal Borough of Greenwich offers a wide range of primary schools, including community schools, academies, and faith schools. Several have received Outstanding ratings from Ofsted. School admissions are primarily distance-based for community schools, creating the familiar catchment area effect on property values — particularly notable in Eltham and Blackheath where the most popular schools are significantly oversubscribed.

Secondary Schools

Secondary school options include Thomas Tallis School in Blackheath (a high-performing comprehensive), Eltham Hill School, Woolwich Polytechnic School, and several academies across the borough. The borough doesn't have grammar schools (unlike neighbouring Bexley), so the comprehensive system serves all areas. Some schools have specialist status in arts, languages, or technology.

Independent Schools

The borough and surrounding areas offer independent school options including Blackheath High School (GDST), Colfe's School in Lee, and Eltham College. These schools draw from across South East London and Kent.

School Impact on Property Values

School quality directly affects property prices across the borough. Properties within catchment of the most sought-after primaries in Eltham and Blackheath can command premiums of 5-15% over comparable homes just outside the boundary. This effect is well documented and consistent.

Transport

Elizabeth Line

The Elizabeth line has transformed connectivity for the eastern part of the borough. Woolwich station provides services reaching Liverpool Street in approximately 15 minutes, Canary Wharf in 7 minutes, Paddington in 30 minutes, and Heathrow in under an hour. Abbey Wood station, on the borough boundary, provides an additional Elizabeth line connection. This has been the most significant transport development in the borough's recent history.

DLR

The Docklands Light Railway serves Greenwich Town, with stations at Cutty Sark and Greenwich providing connections to Canary Wharf, Bank, and the wider DLR network. The proposed extension to Thamesmead, if approved, would be transformative for the eastern part of the borough.

Jubilee Line

North Greenwich station on the Jubilee line serves the Greenwich Peninsula and surrounding areas. Direct services reach London Bridge, Westminster, Bond Street, and Stratford, making it one of the borough's most valuable transport assets.

National Rail

Several National Rail stations serve the borough. Blackheath station offers services to London Bridge (approximately 15 minutes), Charing Cross, and Cannon Street. Eltham and Mottingham stations provide Southeastern services to London Bridge and Charing Cross. Charlton station serves the area between Greenwich and Woolwich. Plumstead station provides connections to Central London via the North Kent line.

Buses

TfL buses provide extensive coverage across the borough, connecting neighbourhoods to each other and to key transport hubs. Routes along major corridors — Woolwich Road, Eltham High Street, Shooters Hill Road — are frequent during peak hours. Night bus services cover key routes through the borough.

River Services

Thames Clipper services operate from Greenwich Pier and North Greenwich Pier, providing a scenic commute to Embankment, Westminster, and other Thames piers. The Woolwich Ferry provides a free vehicle and pedestrian crossing to North Woolwich.

Roads

The A2 runs through the southern part of the borough, connecting to the M25 and the Channel ports. The Blackwall Tunnel (A102) provides a route north of the river to the A12 and M11. The Silvertown Tunnel, currently under construction, will provide a new Thames crossing between Greenwich Peninsula and Silvertown, intended to ease pressure on the Blackwall Tunnel.

Healthcare

Hospitals

Queen Elizabeth Hospital in Woolwich is the borough's main acute hospital, providing A&E, maternity, and a comprehensive range of services. It's operated by Lewisham and Greenwich NHS Trust. University Hospital Lewisham provides additional NHS hospital services for western parts of the borough. For specialist treatments, residents also access services at Guy's and St Thomas' hospitals.

GP Services

Numerous GP practices operate across the borough. The Greenwich Health Centre in Greenwich Town and various practices in Woolwich, Eltham, and other neighbourhoods provide primary care services. As with much of London, some areas face GP capacity pressures, but overall provision is adequate.

Parks and Green Spaces

The Royal Borough of Greenwich is exceptionally well served by green spaces. Greenwich Park (183 acres) is a Royal Park with deer, formal gardens, and spectacular views. Blackheath (267 acres) provides vast open space for recreation and events. Oxleas Wood is an ancient woodland with panoramic London views. Eltham Palace gardens offer formal landscaping and tranquil grounds. Plumstead Common and Woolwich Common provide neighbourhood green spaces. The Thames Path offers riverside walking and cycling along the borough's northern boundary. Maryon Park and Maryon Wilson Park in Charlton provide wooded green space close to the river.

Culture and Leisure

Greenwich's cultural offering is outstanding. The National Maritime Museum and Royal Observatory are world-class institutions. Woolwich Works provides contemporary arts and performance in restored arsenal buildings. The O2 Arena at North Greenwich is one of the world's busiest entertainment venues. Greenwich Theatre, the Bob Hope Theatre in Eltham, and various community venues host regular performances. Greenwich Market, Woolwich Market, and various food markets across the borough add to the cultural mix.

Shopping and Dining

Greenwich Town centre offers independent shops, the famous covered market, and a growing restaurant scene. Woolwich's expanding retail offering includes the Powis Street shopping area and the Royal Arsenal's restaurants and bars. Eltham High Street provides everyday shopping with an increasing number of independent cafes. Blackheath Village offers upmarket independent retailers and restaurants. For major retail trips, Bluewater Shopping Centre in Kent and Westfield Stratford are both accessible.
